notes: In a later revelation, the Lord spoke of the "oracles in your most holy places" (D&C 124:39). Of this meaning, Smith and Sjodahl wrote: "'Oracles' means the place in which the divine revelations are received. The name is applied to the sacred Scriptures, which contain the Word of God, and also to the part of the Temple called the Holy of Holies, where the presence of God was manifested (1 Kgs. 8:6; 2 Chron. 4:20; Ps. 28:2)." (SS, 778.) (Hoyt W. Brewster, Jr., Doctrine and Covenants Encyclopedia, p.399)

In a general sense, any sacred place where oracles are received is called an oracle. A temple is an oracle in this sense, with the holy of holies therein being specifically so designated. (1 Kings 6:16; 8:6; 2 Chron. 4:20; Ps. 28:2.) Sacred revelations or oracles given in such places warrant designating the place itself as an oracle, that is, as a house where revelation is received. (D. & C. 124:39.) (Bruce R. Mc Conkie, Mormon Doctrine, 2d ed., p.547)
oracle (debir) the holy of holies, the innermost room of the temple or tabernacle. That is to say that the hands were being raised to the heavenly throne of God.

Like my Brethren before me, I receive with this calling the assurance that God will direct his prophet. I humbly accept the call to serve and declare with the Psalmist, “The Lord is my strength and my shield; my heart trusted in him, and I am helped” (Psalm 28:7). (Howard W. Hunter, in Conference Report, Oct. 1994, 7; or Ensign, Nov. 1994, 8)

quotes.text: Like my Brethren before me, I receive with this calling the assurance that God will direct his prophet. I humbly accept the call to serve and declare with the Psalmist, “The Lord is my strength and my shield; my heart trusted in him, and I am helped” (Psalm 28:7).
quotes.attr: Howard W. Hunter, in Conference Report, Oct. 1994, 7; or Ensign, Nov. 1994, 8

notes: This psalm was probably composed on the occasion of a thunderstorm.
